documents.title: Acme Corp Q3 2024 Earnings Call
documents.content:
Operator: Good afternoon. Welcome to Acme Corp's third quarter 2024 earnings call. At this time all participants are in listen-only mode. I will now turn the call over to Jane Smith, Head of Investor Relations.

Jane Smith -- Head of IR: Thank you, operator. Joining me today are Maria Chen, CEO, and David Park, CFO. Maria will begin with prepared remarks, then we'll open the line for Q&A. Please note that our remarks contain forward-looking statements. Actual results may differ. We'll also discuss non-GAAP measures; reconciliations are in our 8-K.

Maria Chen -- CEO: Thanks, Jane. Third quarter revenue was $412 million, up 18% year over year. We saw particular strength in enterprise, where we added 120 new logos. Enterprise now represents 68% of total revenue, up from 62% a year ago. International grew 24% and contributed 35% of revenue. Our full-year revenue guidance remains unchanged at $1.62 billion to $1.65 billion. For Q4 we expect revenue of $435 million to $448 million, implying 15% to 18% growth.

Maria Chen -- CEO: On product: we shipped Acme Platform 4.0 in September with the new AI Copilot feature. Early adoption has been strong; 40% of enterprise customers have enabled it. We're investing roughly $50 million this year in AI infrastructure and talent. We expect AI-driven features to contribute meaningfully to revenue starting in the first half of 2025. We're also expanding our sales team; we added 45 reps in Q3 and plan to add another 30 in Q4, primarily in EMEA and APAC.

David Park -- CFO: I'll add color on margins and cash. Non-GAAP operating margin for Q3 was 24%, up 200 basis points from a year ago. Gross margin was 78%, flat sequentially. We're raising full-year operating margin guidance to 23% to 24%, from 22% to 23% previously. Free cash flow was $89 million. We ended the quarter with $1.2 billion in cash and equivalents. CapEx was $32 million, in line with expectations. We repurchased $25 million of stock in Q3. DSO improved to 42 days from 48 a year ago. Inventory came down $8 million sequentially; we're comfortable with levels going into year-end.

David Park -- CFO: On headcount: we ended Q3 with 2,340 employees, up 12% year over year. R&D was 22% of revenue. Sales and marketing was 28%. G&A was 8%.

Operator: We will now begin the question-and-answer session.

Operator: The first question comes from Alex Rivera with Bernstein.

Alex Rivera -- Bernstein: Thanks. Two questions. First, on the competitive landscape in Europe, are you seeing share gains against legacy vendors?

Maria Chen -- CEO: Yes, Alex. In EMEA we're taking share from the incumbents, especially in financial services and retail. Our win rate in head-to-head deals was 64% in Q3, up from 58% last quarter. The main differentiators are our AI Copilot and our unified platform vs. point solutions.

Alex Rivera -- Bernstein: Second, on gross margin. You mentioned 78% flat. What's the path to 80%?

David Park -- CFO: We see gross margin expansion coming from three areas: mix shift toward enterprise and platform services, efficiency gains in our cloud infrastructure as we scale, and pricing on renewals. We're not guiding to 80% yet, but we believe we have a clear path over the next 18 to 24 months.

Operator: The next question comes from Sam Lee with Morgan Stanley.

Sam Lee -- Morgan Stanley: Hi, thanks. Can you quantify the AI Copilot attach rate and what you're seeing on pricing?

Maria Chen -- CEO: Sure. As I mentioned, 40% of enterprise customers have enabled Copilot in the first month. We're charging a 15% premium for the AI tier. Early retention data looks good, but it's too early to call out churn. We'll have more color on the Q4 call.

Sam Lee -- Morgan Stanley: And on the sales rep adds, is the productivity of new reps meeting expectations?

David Park -- CFO: Yes. New rep ramp is about 9 months to full productivity, consistent with prior years. Quota attainment for the cohort that ramped in Q1 is tracking ahead of plan.

Operator: The next question comes from Priya Patel with Goldman Sachs.

Priya Patel -- Goldman Sachs: Thanks. Two quick ones. First, what drove the DSO improvement?

David Park -- CFO: Mainly two things: we tightened payment terms on new enterprise contracts, and we had a higher mix of upfront annual deals in Q3. We're targeting DSO in the high 30s by end of FY25.

Priya Patel -- Goldman Sachs: And on M&A, any update on the pipeline? I know you've been quiet for a while.

Maria Chen -- CEO: We're selectively looking at tuck-ins in AI, security, and vertical-specific capabilities. Nothing to announce. Our priority remains organic growth and the AI build-out. If the right asset comes at the right price, we have the balance sheet to act.

Operator: The next question comes from Mike Chen with JP Morgan.

Mike Chen -- JP Morgan: Thanks. Can you break out the 24% international growth by region? And any color on China given the macro?

Maria Chen -- CEO: EMEA was up 28%, APAC ex-China was up 22%, and China was up 8%. China is still a headwind. We're not assuming improvement there in the near term. The good news is China is only 6% of total revenue, down from 8% a year ago, so the impact is manageable.

Mike Chen -- JP Morgan: And on the stock buyback, $25 million seems light. Is that a signal you're holding firepower for M&A?

David Park -- CFO: No change in our capital allocation priorities. We'll continue to return capital via buybacks while investing in growth. The $25 million was in line with our typical run rate. We don't have a formal buyback program; we repurchase opportunistically.

Operator: The next question comes from Sarah Kim with Citigroup.

Sarah Kim -- Citigroup: Thanks. On the Platform 4.0 launch, any cannibalization of professional services? And how should we think about services mix going forward?

Maria Chen -- CEO: Minimal cannibalization. Services revenue grew 14% in Q3. The platform is more capable out of the box, but customers still need implementation, integration, and change management. We're targeting services at 15% to 18% of total revenue over time, down from 20% today, but that's a multi-year mix shift.

Sarah Kim -- Citigroup: And on competition from the hyperscalers—Azure, AWS—are they showing up more in deals?

Maria Chen -- CEO: They're in the consideration set, especially for cloud-native customers. But we're winning most of those. Our differentiation is we're platform-agnostic; we run on Azure, AWS, GCP, and on-prem. The hyperscalers tend to push their native stacks. We're also seeing more coopetition—we integrate with their AI services. So it's nuanced.

Operator: The final question comes from Tom Wilson with RBC.

Tom Wilson -- RBC: Thanks. Quick one on OpEx. You're adding headcount. How do you see OpEx growth in Q4 and into FY25?

David Park -- CFO: We're guiding Q4 non-GAAP OpEx up roughly 12% to 15% year over year, driven by the sales and R&D hires. For FY25, we'd expect OpEx growth to moderate as we lap the hiring. We're committed to operating leverage; that's why we raised the margin guide.

Tom Wilson -- RBC: And any update on the patent litigation with CompetitorX?

Maria Chen -- CEO: The case is in discovery. We're confident in our position. No material financial impact expected. We don't comment beyond that.

Jane Smith -- Head of IR: Thanks, everyone. That concludes our call. We'll be at the UBS conference next week and the Bernstein conference in December. Have a good afternoon.

Operator: This concludes Acme Corp's earnings call. You may now disconnect.
